topic: Why Concert Tickets Cost So Much (And It's Not the Artist)

packaging.title: Why Concert Tickets Really Cost $400
altTitles: The Real Reason Concert Tickets Cost So Much
How Ticketmaster Quietly Sets the Price
packaging.description: A breakdown of the hidden fee structure, dynamic pricing, and the resale machine that decides what you actually pay for a concert ticket — and why the artist sees almost none of the markup.

thumbnail.visualIdea: A single concert ticket with a giant price tag dangling, the fee line circled and bleeding red

packaging.hookPromise: That $400 ticket isn't expensive because the band is greedy — it's the output of a pricing system designed so you can never tell what the seat is actually worth.
openLoops: Why does the same seat cost three different prices in the same hour?
Where do the 'service fees' actually go, and who splits them?
Why does the artist often make more from a $30 t-shirt than your $400 ticket?
packaging.payoff: The venue, the ticketing platform, and the promoter are frequently the same parent company collecting on both sides — and they intentionally hold back inventory to feed their own resale market at a markup, so the 'sold out in seconds' scarcity is partly manufactured.
packaging.whyItWorks: Nearly everyone has rage-bought an overpriced ticket and blamed the wrong party, so the promise to expose the real machine taps universal frustration with a concrete dollar gap.
